text: The surgeon begins dissection at the peritoneum near the neck of the gallbladder, aiming to define Calot's triangle. The goal is to identify the junction of the cystic duct and the neck of the gallbladder, along with other structures within Calot's triangle.

The surgeon identifies the node of Loon and the cystic artery, continuing dissection along the anterior and posterior peritoneal folds. Fibro-fatty tissue is cleared around the cystic artery within the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection and incision of the posterior peritoneum, utilizing all possible planes to facilitate easier dissection in later stages of the surgery.

The surgeon clears additional fibro-fatty tissue at Calot's triangle, encountering a few small vessels and achieving hemostasis. The cystic duct is observed to be positioned more anteriorly than typical.

The surgeon performs additional clearance posteriorly, defining the cystic artery and the lymph node of Luschka. The dissection ensures a clear tunnel is formed, with careful clearance around these structures to prevent injury.
The assistant uses laparoscopic instruments to apply clips to the cystic artery and cystic duct. The surgeon then divides these structures and proceeds to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed.
The surgeon navigates through minimal adhesions in the surgical field, smoothly dissecting the areolar tissue plane without injuring the gallbladder.
